subject: Economics
paper_name: Edexcel Economics A Paper 2: The National and Global Economy (June 2023)

sections.name: Section A: Data Response — UK Inflation
Section B: Data Response — UK Trade

questions.question_text: With reference to Extract A, identify two significant features of the data shown.
questions.extract_text: Extract A shows UK CPI inflation and Bank of England base rate from 2020 to 2023. CPI inflation rose from 0.7% in January 2021 to a peak of 11.1% in October 2022, before falling to 6.7% by September 2023. The Bank of England base rate was held at 0.1% until December 2021, then increased 14 consecutive times to reach 5.25% by August 2023. Average real wages fell by 3.1% in the year to March 2023.

questions.question_text: With reference to the information provided, explain how cost-push factors contributed to the inflationary pressures experienced by the UK economy.
With reference to Extract B and your own knowledge, assess the effectiveness of monetary policy in controlling inflation in the UK.
questions.extract_text: Extract B: The Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee raised interest rates from 0.1% to 5.25% between December 2021 and August 2023. Mortgage approvals fell by 28% in the year to July 2023. However, inflation remained above the 2% target throughout 2023. Some economists argue that monetary policy is less effective when inflation is driven by supply-side shocks rather than excess demand. Business investment fell by 1.2% in Q2 2023.

questions.question_text: With reference to the extracts and your own knowledge, evaluate the trade-offs the Bank of England faces when setting interest rates during a period of high inflation and low economic growth.
Evaluate the view that fiscal policy would be more effective than monetary policy in managing the UK economy during the current macroeconomic environment.
With reference to Extract C, identify two significant features of the data shown.
questions.extract_text: Extract C shows the UK's balance of trade in goods and services from 2018 to 2023. The trade deficit in goods widened from £139bn in 2018 to £186bn in 2023. The trade surplus in services grew from £108bn to £132bn over the same period. The overall current account deficit was £83bn (3.1% of GDP) in 2023. UK exports to the EU fell by 7% between 2019 and 2023, while exports to non-EU countries grew by 4%.

questions.question_text: With reference to the information provided, explain how a depreciating exchange rate might affect the UK's balance of trade.
With reference to Extract D and your own knowledge, assess the impact of increased trade barriers on the UK economy since 2021.
questions.extract_text: Extract D: Since leaving the EU single market in January 2021, UK firms have faced new customs checks, regulatory divergence, and rules of origin requirements on exports to the EU. A 2023 study estimated that these barriers reduced UK-EU trade by approximately 15% compared to a scenario where the UK had remained in the single market. However, the UK has signed new trade agreements with Australia, New Zealand, and is seeking CPTPP membership. Small and medium-sized enterprises have been disproportionately affected, with 24% of SMEs that previously exported to the EU ceasing to do so.

questions.question_text: With reference to the extracts and your own knowledge, evaluate whether the UK's comparative advantage has changed as a result of its new trading relationships.
Evaluate the view that free trade always leads to net economic benefits for all participating countries.
Evaluate the effectiveness of supply-side policies in promoting long-run economic growth in the UK. Refer to at least one diagram in your answer.
Evaluate the view that a floating exchange rate system is always preferable to a fixed exchange rate system for the UK. Refer to at least one diagram in your answer.
